response:
The fare depends on the total distance which includes both driving and converted waiting time. This rule creates a spring of pressure for drivers to keep moving during every trip. The relationship between fare $\eta$ and distance $\xi$ is defined by a base fee plus extra charges for distance beyond four kilometers. If the distance is four or less then the cost stays at ten yuan for the entire ride. When the distance exceeds four kilometers the additional cost is two yuan per extra kilometer rounded up. This pricing structure acts like a spring that pushes passengers to choose shorter routes whenever possible. The formula for the fare when distance is greater than four is ten plus two times the ceiling of the distance minus four.

A passenger paying thirty eight yuan implies the total calculated distance was exactly nineteen kilometers. Since the actual driving distance was fifteen kilometers the difference of four kilometers must come from waiting time. The city rule states that five minutes of stopping equals one kilometer of driving distance for fare calculation. Therefore four kilometers of converted distance means the taxi stopped for twenty minutes at most. This waiting limit ensures the fare does not exceed the amount the passenger actually paid. The concept of converting time to distance works like a spring that balances driver income with passenger cost. Any stop longer than twenty minutes would have increased the fare beyond the thirty eight yuan limit. Drivers must manage their time carefully to avoid triggering higher charges for their passengers.
